domain:
ꗝ})**Glossary**:
  • Human intelligence: Studied in-depth in many fields of science such as philosophy, linguistics, and neuroscience; defined by psychology through a set of theories and characteristics, and assessed via tests that measure the level of intellectual development.
  • Animal intelligence: Mostly related to learning ability (e.g., in monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows); more challenging to measure than human intelligence due to greater animal diversity regarding cognitive and perceptual capacities, available sensory organs, and ways they explore their environment.
  • Plant intelligence: Intelligence not based on a brain and nervous system; manifests through learning based on repeated practice, memory, the ability to percept multiple aspects (shape, colour, smell and sound) simultaneously, a “sense of place,” and an awareness of the neighbourhood. It involves continually gathering and updating information about its environment, integrating this with internal state, and making decisions to reconcile well-being with the environment.
  • Intelligence: A concept that should be defined based on principles which are fundamental and unlikely to alter over time, not limited to any particular set of senses, environments, goals, or implementation structures, and encompass the essence of different types of intelligence; ideally it should be formally expressed, objective, and practically realisable as an effective test.
  • Artificial intelligence: Historically considered in a strong connection with human intelligence. According to John McCarthy (1956), it is based on the conjecture that every aspect of learning or any other feature of intelligence can be so precisely described that a machine can be made to simulate it, allowing machines to use language, form abstractions and concepts, solve problems now reserved for humans, and improve themselves.
  • Intelligent computer systems: Systems designed to support people in the completion of different functions and activities that people in the real world perform or know how to perform.
  • Rationality: An ideal intelligence used as a standard for certain categories of artificial intelligence definitions.
  • Cognitive modelling approach: An approach underlying systems that think humanly; it considers human being to be an information processing system and focuses on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers.
  • Systems that think rationally: Systems based on logics where knowledge is represented as logical rules to infer new knowledge.
  • Turing test approach: An approach that underlies systems that act humanly.
**Note**: A general definition of intelligence does not currently exist due to limited knowledge of the world, which consequently means a definitive definition of artificial intelligence and a standard for assessing if a computer system possesses intelligence is also lacking.**Note 2**: Definitions of AI are grouped into four categories by Russell and Norvig (2009), divided by thought processes/reasoning versus behaviour, and by using either human intelligence or rationality as the standard.**Note 3**: Human intelligence is problematic to measure due to diversity determined by linguistic, cultural, physical condition and other differences, and many psychological tests are static, failing to measure ability to learn and adapt over time.**Note 4**: Animal intelligence studies may be biased if they expect animal intelligence to be based on the same principles as human intelligence.**Note 5**: Plant intelligence is characterized by the absence of a brain and nervous system.**Note 6**: The term

glossary.definition: systems being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or, in other words, to cope with different types of problems.
glossary.sentence: At the beginning of the study course, you learnt out that intelligent computer systems are being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or, in other words, to cope with different types of problems.

glossary.definition: a way of structural organization of data about the real world
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: data structures or, in other words, a way of structural organization of data about the real world;

glossary.definition: places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others
glossary.sentence: data repositories or places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others;

glossary.definition: ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal
glossary.sentence: algorithms or ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.

glossary.definition: also called a formal specification; uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.
glossary.sentence: We need a formal or mathematical representation, also called a formal specification (Figure 3). The formal representation uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.

glossary.definition: a formal or mathematical representation that uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.
glossary.sentence: We need a formal or mathematical representation, also called a formal specification (Figure 3).

glossary.definition: determining a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states.
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4).

glossary.term: initial state of the problem-solving process
glossary.definition: a state we are in at present during the definition of a problem.
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two the same as initial state

glossary.term: goal state of the problem-solving process
glossary.definition: a state we would like to reach during the definition of a problem.
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in the same as goal state

glossary.definition: a set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.
glossary.sentence: A state is a set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.

glossary.definition: A graph that can be complete or partial. In a complete state space graph, minimax and alpha-beta pruning are applied; in a partial state space graph, results cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.
glossary.sentence: Although we have applied the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning to a complete state space graph in this game, these principles can also be used in strategies for processing a partial state space graph.

glossary.definition: Another name for single neurons.
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes.

glossary.definition: A node that has no ancestor.
glossary.sentence: However, there is no ancestor for the root node, and the algorithm therefore goes down again, via the first unconsidered path.

glossary.definition: a node which corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process; from the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have descendants.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph should have at least one terminal (leaf) node , which corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have descendants;

glossary.definition: nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process; from the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that have both ancestors and descendants.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph may have internal nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that have both ancestors and descendants.

glossary.definition: Elements of a state space graph used to represent legal moves in the game.
glossary.sentence: Its nodes correspond to discrete states of the game, and arcs are used to represent legal moves in the game.

glossary.definition: A process that can be seen as playing a game, where an initial state is moved toward goal states through moves.
glossary.sentence: Playing a game can be seen as a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: The starting point of the problem-solving process; for example, in tic-tac-toe, it is a blank grid.
glossary.sentence: In this game, the initial state of the problem-solving process is a blank grid, which appears when you start the game (Figure 3).

glossary.definition: States in which three marks corresponding to that player are located in a horizontal, vertical or a diagonal row in tic-tac-toe.
glossary.sentence: Each player wants to reach one of the goal states, in which three marks corresponding to that player are located in a horizontal, vertical or a diagonal row (Figure 4).

glossary.definition: A specific state produced by each of the possible moves from the initial state.
glossary.sentence: Each of the possible moves produces a specific intermediate state.

glossary.definition: States that players change between by making legal moves in the game.
glossary.sentence: Thus, playing a game involves changing between game states by making legal moves in the game.

glossary.term: deterministic two-player game of perfect information
glossary.definition: A type of game where moves are made alternately, node levels in the state space graph are clearly distinguishable, and it follows the principle of a zero-sum deterministic two-player game of perfect information.
glossary.sentence: Since moves are made alternately in a deterministic two-player game of perfect information, node levels are clearly distinguishable in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: Levels in the state space graph where one or more nodes are located at each level, displaying states of the game from which only one of the two players can make a move.
glossary.sentence: One or more nodes are located at each level, and these display states of the game from which only one of the two players can make a move.

glossary.term: zero-sum deterministic two-player game of perfect information
glossary.definition: A type of game where it is contradicted if the same player could make two moves rather than only one at the same level of the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: If an arc was drawn between two nodes at the same level, this would indicate that the same player could make two moves rather than only one, which would contradict the principle of a zero-sum deterministic two-player game of perfect information as defined at the beginning of this topic.

glossary.definition: Values that characterise the states in the game; they are related to a computer implementation of a game, correspond to the properties of the game and its course (changing after a move), and allow players to track the course of the game by determining a specific state at a specific moment in time. They are not related to characteristics of the state space graph such as number of nodes, levels or arcs.
glossary.sentence: As a result, before creating a state space graph, the developer of an intelligent computer system should determine the parameters that characterise the states in the game .

glossary.term: complexity of a state space graph
glossary.definition: A critical aspect that must be reduced to avoid overconsumption of computational resources.
glossary.sentence: The complexity of a state space graph is a critical aspect.

glossary.definition: Nodes at the same level that have the same values for all of the parameters that characterise the states of the game.
glossary.sentence: Nodes at the same level are identical if they have the same values for all of the parameters that characterise the states of the game.

glossary.definition: Nodes in which there is only one number left in the numerical string in the example oversimplified game.
glossary.sentence: We can continue in the same way until we reach leaf nodes, in which there is only one number left in the numerical string.

glossary.definition: an algorithm that performs three main steps: selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node
glossary.sentence: In the previous topic of the course, you have already considered that any search algorithm performs three main steps: selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node.

glossary.definition: a system that needs a search algorithm that processes the data structure to find a solution to the problem based on known input data
glossary.sentence: Additionally, an intelligent computer system needs a search algorithm that processes the data structure to find a solution to the problem based on known input data (Figure 1).

glossary.definition: A path found by a search algorithm in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The search principles explained previously have shown that the search algorithm sequentially explores different paths in the state space graph. This process may result in several situations that determine how fast and whether the search algorithm will find a solution path.

glossary.definition: A known node in the state space graph for the search purpose.
glossary.sentence: The search start node and the search goal node are known for the search purpose.

glossary.definition: definition's value is not provided in the text. The search start node and the search goal node are known for the search purpose.
glossary.sentence: The search start node and the search

glossary.definition: A situation where nodes are connected in a way that can make a search algorithm get stuck and never find the search goal node (e.g., node H connected to node D, which is connected to node C).
glossary.sentence: the node H is connected to the node D, which is connected to the node C, together making a cycle.

glossary.definition: A meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph. If the search goal node is not reached by the current path, it backtracks to the most recent node on the path having unexamined descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction) and tries another path.
glossary.sentence: Backtracking is nowadays considered to be a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph...

glossary.definition: A node that can be reached by going down from an intermediate state.
glossary.sentence: It reaches a leaf node in which the minimiser has the highest score and therefore wins the game.

glossary.definition: Search algorithms that cannot distinguish between paths that allow them to reach the search goal node and paths that do not.
glossary.sentence: Uninformed search algorithms cannot distinguish between paths that allow them to reach the search goal node and paths that do not.

glossary.definition: Another name for uninformed search algorithms.
glossary.sentence: This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search...

glossary.definition: Another name for uninformed search algorithms.
glossary.sentence: This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search...

glossary.definition: An algorithm that explores the state space graph through a principle of deepening.
glossary.sentence: Both algorithms considered so far—Depth-First Search and Depth-Limited Search—explored the state space graph through a principle of deepening.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths, but is not able to find a solution path if the search goal node is located below the depth limit.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths. At the same time, it is not able to find a solution path if the search goal node is located below the depth limit.

glossary.definition: uninformed search algorithm example
glossary.sentence: In this course, we will consider Depth-First Search, Depth-Limited Search, Breadth-First Search, and Bidirectional Search as examples of algorithms in this category.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions; it consumes less computational resources than Breadth-First Search and is able to find the shortest path to the search goal node, but requires knowing exactly the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions.

glossary.definition: Algorithms whose effectiveness of finding the search goal node can be improved by using heuristic knowledge; in state space search, they provide rules for choosing paths in a state space graph that are most likely to lead to an acceptable problem solution.
glossary.sentence: The effectiveness of finding the search goal node can be improved by using heuristic search algorithms.

glossary.definition: An example of a heuristic search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Hill Climbing, Best-First Search, and Beam Search will be considered as examples of algorithms in this category in our course.

glossary.definition: An example of a heuristic search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Hill Climbing, Best-First Search, and Beam Search will be considered as examples of this category in our course.

glossary.definition: An example of a heuristic search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Hill Climbing, Best-First Search, and Beam Search will be considered as examples of algorithms in this category in our course.

glossary.definition: a system that will advise tourists about the selection of roads between two cities in France: Paris and Toulouse
glossary.sentence: Imagine that we are building a recommendation system that will advise tourists about the selection of roads between two cities in France: Paris and Toulouse.

glossary.definition: search algorithms that process only nodes and arcs of the state space graph and do not have additional information about the quality of the nodes
glossary.sentence: In the case of uninformed search, all of the paths represented in the state space graph are identical, because uninformed search algorithms process only nodes and arcs of the state space graph. They do not have additional information about the quality of the nodes.

glossary.definition: a data structure that stores a set of elements in sequential order
glossary.sentence: Search algorithms are often implemented using a data structure called a list. A list stores a set of elements in sequential order.

glossary.definition: A list where new nodes are added to the right side in Breadth-First Search; it functions as a queue data structure following the “First in, First Out” principle.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the right side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a queue.

glossary.definition: A list that contains nodes that have already been expanded.
glossary.sentence: The CLOSED list is empty because none of the available nodes has been expanded so far.

glossary.definition: a type of data structure that works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the left side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a stack. A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: A search method that explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches; its main problem is the consumption of computational resources (search time and computer memory).
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches.

glossary.definition: A type of data structure that works according to the “First in, First Out” principle: the first data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a queue. A queue works according to the “First in, First Out” principle: the first data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 18).

glossary.definition: The node currently being processed or checked by the algorithm, often located on the first position of the OPEN list.
glossary.sentence: The node K, which is located on the first position of the OPEN list, becomes the current node.

glossary.definition: One of the two directions in which Bidirectional Search performs a search simultaneously.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions.

glossary.definition: One of the two directions in which Bidirectional Search performs a search simultaneously.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm performs simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions.

glossary.definition: Lists used by the Bidirectional Search algorithm; the algorithm checks if there is at least one common node in both OPEN lists to determine if a point of contact has been found.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists.

glossary.definition: Lists used by the Bidirectional Search algorithm; nodes are moved to these lists once they have been expanded.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists.

glossary.definition: Experience-based rules that allow people to solve problems, make decisions, and reason in a more effective way; it is described as a rule of thumb that usually leads to a good or at least acceptable outcome with much less effort, though not necessarily the perfect outcome.
glossary.sentence: Heuristics (or heuristic knowledge) are experience-based rules that allow people to solve problems, make decisions, and reason in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: A mathematical function used in intelligent computer systems to represent heuristic knowledge. This function helps the search algorithm to determine the direction of the location of the search goal node and its purpose is to find a problem solution with minimum costs.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic knowledge in intelligent computer systems is represented as a mathematical function called a heuristic evaluation function .

glossary.definition: Problem-specific knowledge that very often is based on our experience. It is used for solving complex problems with incomplete information when it is not possible to describe or evaluate the problem in full detail, and for solving problems that may not have an exact solution because of inherent ambiguities in the problem statement or available data, or problems that may have an exact solution but there are not enough computational resources to solve them. In intelligent computer systems, it is represented as a mathematical function called a heuristic evaluation function.
glossary.sentence: To solve problems, we use problem-specific knowledge called heuristic knowledge that very often is based on our experience.

glossary.definition: Numerical values calculated by the heuristic evaluation function and assigned to nodes of the state space graph. They represent the quality of the nodes.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic knowledge is incorporated in the heuristic evaluation function, which calculates numerical values called heuristic measures .

glossary.definition: An abstract representation where f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n is a node under consideration.
glossary.sentence: This idea is represented in an abstract way as follows (Luger, 2009): f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration;

glossary.definition: The distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available.
glossary.sentence: g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available;

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: sentence: h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.

glossary.definition: The weight of the ith factor.
glossary.sentence: ai – the weight of the ith factor;

glossary.definition: The heuristic measure of the ith factor characterizing states in the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: xi – the heuristic measure of the ith factor characterizing states in the problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: A value assigned by the algorithm that may be checked against the ancestor of the current node in the current path to determine if a cut-off should be performed.
glossary.sentence: When the algorithm has assigned an intermediate heuristic measure, it checks whether the ancestor of the current node in the current path also has an intermediate heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node in a specific scenario described in Step 14.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure of the ancestor in a specific scenario described in Step 14.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: An action performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node when the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor.
glossary.sentence: meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A value assigned to a node after its unconsidered paths were cut off, based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far; or a value assigned when a node does not have other unconsidered descendants, based on the level of the current node and the known heuristic measures of the direct descendants.
glossary.sentence: After making the cut off, the node whose unconsidered paths were cut off acquires a precise heuristic measure based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far.

glossary.definition: A level where the current node acquires a heuristic measure based on the descendants' measures when there is no difference between them.
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MIN level, but there is no difference between the heuristic measures of the descendants.

glossary.definition: A node at this level may be assigned an intermediate heuristic measure (e.g., >=1 or >=-1) because it is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure when there are other unconsidered descendants; the task of the maximiser at this level is to win the game.
glossary.sentence: since the node is located at the MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: The entity whose task is to win the game.
glossary.sentence: since the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: The entity whose task is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, i.e. to prevent the maximiser from winning the game.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case the minimiser will take the measure of −1, but in the best case, the measure will be lower, since the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, i.e. to prevent the maximiser from winning the game.

glossary.definition: An action performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node when the value of β is smaller than α.
glossary.sentence: meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: An algorithm whose game results are compared to those of alpha-beta pruning and provide identical winning paths.
glossary.sentence: This is a game result, and we can see that it is the same as provided by the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: A process where nodes are excluded from evaluation and branches are cut off to improve efficiency in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: A more interesting issue is the efficiency of alpha-beta pruning.

glossary.definition: A more complex case of a state space graph where the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.
glossary.sentence: A partial state space graph is a more complex case, since the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.

glossary.definition: One of two approaches used to process a partial state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The two approaches of N-ply look-ahead and iterative deepening are used to process a partial state space graph (Figure 11).

glossary.definition: One of two approaches used to process a partial state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The two approximations of N-ply look-ahead and iterative deepening are used to process a partial state space graph (Figure 11).

glossary.definition: Carried out when labels of data objects are presented as real values (so they are continuous) as the price of a house, the number of graduating students, etc. For a new data object, the machine learning algorithm finds a prediction.
glossary.sentence: Regression is carried out when labels of data objects are presented as real values (so they are continuous) as the price of a house, the number of graduating students, etc.

glossary.definition: a type of learning that uses unlabelled datasets and reveals the structure of data.
glossary.sentence: unsupervised learning uses unlabelled datasets and reveals the structure of data.

glossary.definition: a process of dividing an available dataset (data objects represented by feature vectors) into subsets that share some similarities.
glossary.sentence: One of the tasks of unsupervised machine learning is clustering . It is a process of dividing an available dataset (data objects represented by feature vectors) into subsets that share some similarities.

glossary.definition: Finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects.
glossary.sentence: Association analysis finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects (Pant, 2019), for example, running an association rule analysis on your sales logs may reveal that people who purchase barbecue sauce and potato chips also tend to buy steak (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: Associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features.
glossary.sentence: Dimensionality reduction is associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: A type of learning where the mathematical model is created based on input data that already contain labels representing the desired solution or answer. It is explicit (the teacher teaches a child) and its task is to infer a mathematical function describing a relationship between input data (feature values) and output.
glossary.sentence: Table 1 compares supervised and unsupervised learning.

glossary.definition: The type of models provided by supervised learning (as indicated in Table 1).
glossary.sentence: Table 1. Supervised learning vs Unsupervised learning Criterion Type of models Predictive Descriptive

glossary.definition: The type of models provided by unsupervised learning.
glossary.sentence: Unsupervised learning provides descriptive models.

glossary.definition: Reward-based learning. Suitable for agents (like robots, self-driving cars, or a game-playing software) who operate in a changing environment and need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal.
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning is reward-based learning.

glossary.definition: Entities such as robots, self-driving cars, or game-playing software that operate in a changing environment and learn how to act by receiving feedback (a reward if it acts correctly and a penalty if it acts incorrectly).
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning is suitable for agents (like robots, self-driving cars, or a game-playing software) who operate in a changing environment...

glossary.definition: Developing a mathematical model of data.
glossary.sentence: Summarising the content of this topic, it is worth emphasising again that machine learning is about developing a mathematical model of data.

glossary.definition: Representing the desired solution/ answer/ output in training data for supervised learning (as indicated in Table 1).
glossary.sentence: Task Based on the training data that includes feature values together with labels representing the desired solution/ answer/ output...

glossary.definition: Deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs.
glossary.sentence: classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs;

glossary.definition: The data processing that leads to a specific output: in a classification task – a class label, in a regression task - a prediction of the value of a specified property of a new data object.
glossary.sentence: According to (Tamir, 2020), a supervised machine learning algorithm includes three components : a decision process: the data processing that leads to a specific output...

glossary.definition: Measuring how good the output is by comparing it to known examples.
glossary.sentence: According to (Tamir, 2020), a supervised machine learning algorithm includes three components : a decision process: ... an error function: measuring how good the output is by comparing it to known examples...

glossary.definition: Reducing the classification or prediction error by updating variables of the mathematical model used by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: e.g., an updating or optimisation process: reducing the classification or prediction error by updating variables of the mathematical model used by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: An algorithm belonging to the category of supervised machine learning algorithms.
glossary.sentence: This category of machine learning algorithms includes many algorithms, for example, Support Vector Machines...

glossary.definition: An algorithm belonging to the category of supervised machine learning algorithms.
glossary.sentence: This category of machine learning algorithms includes many algorithms, for example, k-Nearest Neighbours...

glossary.definition: An algorithm belonging to the category of supervised machine linha own's algorithms.
glossary.sentence: This category of machine learning algorithms includes many algorithms, for example, Decision Trees...

glossary.definition: An algorithm belonging to the category of supervised machine learning algorithms.
glossary.sentence: This category of machine learning algorithms includes many the la l's algorithms, for example, Linear Regression...

glossary.definition: An algorithm belonging to the category of supervised machine learning algorithms.
glossary.sentence: This category of machine learning algorithms includes many algorithms, for example, ... Neural Networks...

glossary.sentence: This category of machine learning algorithms includes many algorithms, for example, ... Logistic Regression...

glossary.definition: An algorithm belonging to the category of supervised machine learning algorithms.
glossary.sentence: This category of machine learning algorithms includes many algorithms, for example, Naive Bayes...

glossary.definition: An algorithm belonging to the category of
glossary.sentence: This category of machine learning algorithms includes many algorithms, for example, ... Random Forest...

glossary.definition: An algorithm belonging to the category of supervised machine learning algorithms.
Values defined by the developer of a machine learning model when using existing tools and libraries.
glossary.sentence: Using the existing tools and libraries, the developer of a machine learning model loads data and defines hyperparameters but rarely needs to write a machine learning algorithm from scratch.

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression where there is a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using specific equations.
glossary.sentence: For univariate linear regression, there is a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using the following equations (Russell and Norvig, 2010):

glossary.definition: A very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.
glossary.sentence: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.

glossary.definition: the hyperparameter of the K-Means algorithm representing the number of clusters that need to be generated.
glossary.sentence: Specify the number of clusters, K , that need to be generated by this algorithm. Thus, K is the hyperparameter of this algorithm.

glossary.definition: an algorithm that calculates the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and current data objects from the dataset, stores indices and distances in a list, sorts them in ascending order, picks top k data objects, gets their labels, and identifies the output for the new data object based on either the mode (for classification tasks) or mean (of the k labels for regression tasks). It typically demands normalization of feature value, does not have a separate training phase, uses all data in the dataset for making predictions, and has no assumptions about data distribution.
glossary.sentence: Further, for each data object in the dataset, the algorithm will (Fig. 17): calculate the distance... identify the output for the new data object: in the classification task, the algorithm will return the mode of the k labels (the most frequent class); in the regression task, it returns the mean of the k labels.

glossary.definition: a method used to calculate the distance between a new data object and current data objects from the dataset; also used for continuous feature values to determine mutual similarity.
glossary.sentence: calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset

glossary.definition: a method used to calculate the distance between a new data object and current data objects from the dataset; also used for continuous feature values to determine mutual similarity.
glossary.sentence: calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset

glossary.definition: a method used to calculate the distance between a new data object and current data objects from the dataset; specifically for categorical feature values, it counts the number of features in which two data objects differ.
glossary.sentence: for categorical feature values, usually, the Hamming distance is used that counts the number of features in which two data objects differ

glossary.definition: a task where the kNN algorithm returns the mode of the k labels (the most frequent class) to identify the output for a new data object.
glossary.sentence: in the classification task, the algorithm will return the mode of the k labels (the most frequent class)

glossary.definition: a task where the kNN algorithm returns the mean of the k labels to identify the output for a new data object.
glossary.sentence: in the regression task, it returns the mean of the k labels.

glossary.definition: subsets of data created during clustering; each cluster is made of one or more data objects and characterized by the similarity of data objects within the cluster and the difference of data objects between the clusters.
glossary.sentence: These subsets of data are called clusters ; for example, an enterprise can group its clients based on the level of income... Therefore, each cluster is made of one or more data objects and characterised by two aspects: the similarity of data objects within the cluster; the difference of data objects between the clusters.

glossary.definition: a concept that depends on feature types: for categorical feature values, it is determined by Hamming distance (fewer differences in features means greater similarity); for continuous feature values, it is based on geometric distance calculated via Euclidean or Manhattan distance (closer data objects mean greater mutual similarity).
glossary.sentence: Therefore, it is worth reminding that the concept of similarity depends on the feature types: for categorical feature values... for continuous feature values, the geometric distance between any pair of data objects is calculated based on the Euclidean or Manhattan distance

glossary.definition: a popular algorithm of unsupervised machine learning based on two central concepts: the concept of distance and the concept of a centroid. The task of K-Means is to minimise the sum of distances between data objects belonging to a cluster and the cluster centroid.
glossary.sentence: The K-Means algorithm is one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning... The task of K-Means is to minimise the sum of distances between data objects belonging to a cluster and the cluster centroid.

glossary.definition: a similarity measure used to group data objects in clusters.
glossary.sentence: A distance represents a similarity measure used to group data objects in clusters (see above).

glossary.definition: a centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid; it is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster.
glossary.sentence: A centroid is a centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid. It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: an unsupervised machine learning algorithm that does not demand any assumption on the number of clusters; it involves building a hierarchy of clusters where clusters at each level are created by merging/dividing clusters at the next lower level, with single data objects as clusters at the lowest level and one cluster containing all data objects at the highest level.
glossary.sentence: Hierarchical clustering is another unsupervised machine learning algorithm that does not demand from the developer any assumption on the number of clusters... Hierarchical clustering is about building a hierarchy of clusters that is characterised by the following aspects...

glossary.definition: a bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters.
glossary.sentence: Agglomerative hierarchical clustering is a bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters.

glossary.definition: a type of hierarchical clustering (mentioned as one of two types).
glossary.sentence: There are two types of hierarchical clustering (Hastie, 2017) : agglomerative hierarchical clustering; divisive hierarchical clustering.

glossary.term:
>
  • Precision: A metric that evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives, or the accuracy of the positive predictions; it is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive. Its value lies in the range [0,1], where a larger value indicates better predictive accuracy.
  • Macro average precision: In multiclass classification, this is calculated by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes.
  • Recall (Sensitivity/True Positive Rate): A metric that evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects; it is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm. Its value lies in the range [0,1], where a larger value indicates better predictive accuracy.
  • Macro average recall: In multiclass classification, this is calculated by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.
  • Precision/recall tradeoff: The phenomenon where increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa.
  • F1 score: A metric used in classification tasks representing the harmonic mean of precision and recall; it estimates the number of data objects classified correctly. Its value lies in the range [0,1], where a high F-score indicates that precision and recall are high and close to each other, indicating better model performance.
  • Macro F1: In multiclass classification, this can be calculated by the macro average precision and recall.
  • Hyperparameters: Tuning these can affect (at least to some extent) the classifier’s behaviour.
  • ROC curve (The receiver operating characteristic): A visualization of the behavior of the classifier under different values of its parameters; it plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping to visualize values that would be good choices for the tradeoff. The ideal case is a curve reaching the upper-left corner (zero error rate on false positives and 100% accuracy on true positives), while the worst-case scenario (random chance) is a 45 degrees diagonal line.
  • Area under the ROC Curve (AUC): A single-number summary of the ROC curve that measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the entire ROC curve; it returns a value in the range [0, 1]. A highly accurate classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 1, while a purely random classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 0.5.
  • Clustering task: A task of unsupervised machine learning that demands dividing data objects into representative groups; its output is groups of data objects and there are no labelling of data objects.
  • Silhouette Score: A metric for clustering performance that does not demand ground-truth labels, representing the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters. It is calculated using average intra-cluster distance (a - the average distance between each point within a cluster) and average inter-cluster distance (b - the average distance between all clusters). Its values lie in the range [-1, 1], where values closer to +1 indicate better separated clusters, 0 indicates that data objects lie on the decision boundary because the distance between neighbouring clusters is not significant, and negative values indicate that data objects might have been assigned to the wrong cluster.
    1. Accuracy: A performance metric; in some cases, such as with imbalanced datasets (e.g., 100 objects where 95 belong to Class 0 and 5 to Class 1), it may not be the right metric to evaluate a model's power because a

  • Macro average recall: In multiclass classification, this is calculated by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.
  • Macro F1: In multiclass classification, this can be calculated by the macro average precision and recall.
  • Precision/recall tradeoff: The phenomenon where increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa.
  • F1 score: A metric used in classification tasks representing the harmonic mean of precision and recall; it estimates the number of data objects classified correctly. Its value lies in the range [0,1], where a high F-score indicates that precision and recall are high and close to each other, indicating better model performance.

The ideal case is a curve reaching the upper-left corner (zero error rate on false positives and 100% accuracy on true positives), while the worst-case scenario (random chance) is a 45 degrees diagonal line.
  • Area under the ROC Curve (AUC): A single-number summary of the ROC curve that measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the entire ROC curve; it returns a value in the range [0, 1]. A highly accurate classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 1, while a purely random classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 0.5.
  • Clustering task: A task of unsupervised machine learning that demands dividing data objects into representative groups; its output is groups of data objects and there are no labelling of data objects.
  • Silhouette Score: A metric for clustering performance that does not demand ground-truth labels, representing the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters. It is calculated using average intra-cluster distance (a - the average distance between each point within a cluster) and average inter-cluster distance (b - the average distance between all clusters). Its values lie in the range [-1, 1], where values closer to +1 indicate better separated clusters, 0 indicates that data objects lie on the decision boundary because the distance between neighbouring clusters is not significant, and negative values indicate that data objects might have been assigned to the wrong cluster.

glossary.definition: Usually plotted on the Silhouette Plot, representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: A function used for combining input signals.
glossary.sentence: We will use the summation function further in this topic. / a function for combining input signals (for example, the summation function )

glossary.definition: Determines the output of the neuron.
glossary.sentence: The transfer (activation) function determines the output of the neuron.

glossary.definition: Also called hard limit functions; often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Also called hard limit functions; often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Another name for step and sign activation functions; often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern.
glossary.sentence: An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern (Callan, 2003; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Another name for single neurons.
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes.

glossary.definition: Neurons connected to the external environment that do not contain computing neurons because they only receive inputs signals from the environment and pass them to the hidden layers.
glossary.sentence: The neurons connected to the external environment form input and output layers. / The input layer does not contain computing neurons because it only receives inputs signals from the environment and passes them to the hidden layers.

glossary.definition: Neurons connected to the external environment; contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions.
glossary.sentence: The neurons connected to the external environment form input and output layers. / On the other hand, the output and hidden layers contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions.

glossary.definition: Layers of neurons between input and output layers; contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions.
glossary.sentence: The layers of neurons between input and output layers are called hidden layers . / On the other hand, the output and hidden layers contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions.

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network that has connections only in one direction; every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units with no loops.
glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir • ection . Every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units; there are no loops.

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network that feeds its outputs back into its inputs.
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs.

glossary.definition: Related to modifying weights in some particular way.
glossary.sentence: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way .

glossary.definition: Applied to modify the weights of the network if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network.
glossary.sentence: In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network.

glossary.definition: Also called graceful degradation; refers to artificial neural networks being resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units where network operation changes very little.
glossary.sentence: According to ( Borisovs et al., 1998), at the same time, artificial neural networks are resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units and network operation changes very little. It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation...

glossary.definition: Another name for fault tolerance.
glossary.sentence: It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation...

glossary.definition: Identifying the structure or pattern in data.
glossary.sentence: pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data;

glossary.definition: Identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects.
glossary.sentence: prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects;

glossary.definition: Finding the "best" organization of constraints.
glossary.sentence: optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints;

glossary.definition: Separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal.
glossary.sentence: noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal;

glossary.definition: Determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables.
glossary.sentence: non-linear estimation determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables and others.

glossary.definition: A model developed by Rosenblatt in the late 1950s.
glossary.sentence: Perceptron Rosenblatt developed the perceptron model in the late 1950s (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.
glossary.sentence: A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.definition: A system where learning is based on assigning initial random values to weights, selecting a training example from the training set, performing feed-forwarding, and performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network until reaching the weights of the input layer.
glossary.sentence: The learning in multilayer perceptron is based on the following steps (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): assigning initial random values to weights; selecting a training example from the training set; performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network; performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer

glossary.definition: Propagation of inputs forward in the network from the input layer to the output layer.
glossary.sentence: performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network; ... The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: The process of propagating error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer.
glossary.sentence: performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer

glossary.definition: An algorithm that allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer, consisting of two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm . It includes two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation (Fig. ... The backpropagation algorithm allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer

glossary.definition: A task where the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels.
glossary.sentence: In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels.

glossary.definition: The difference between the right output and the predicted output.
glossary.sentence: Thus, there is a difference between the right output and the predicted output. It is called an error.

glossary.definition: The theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems; more precisely, an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system.
glossary.sentence: In general, “ knowledge representation is the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems” (Jones, 2009). It can be defined more precisely as an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system (Graham and Barrett, 1997).

glossary.definition: An intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine.
glossary.sentence: A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: A place where knowledge is stored; its structure and the order of production rules determine how to search it and how quickly it will be searched.
glossary.sentence: The structure and the order of production rules in the knowledge base determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.

glossary.definition: The processing mechanism for knowledge stored in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine.

glossary.definition: The inference engine chosen if a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred.
glossary.sentence: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: The inference engine chosen if your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it.
glossary.sentence: However, if your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it, choose the backward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: A natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...”. They are independent units of knowledge whose structure and sequence are significant in the context of a chosen conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...”

glossary.definition: The method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The set of enabled rules from which one rule is chosen to fire during conflict resolution.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A method of conflict resolution based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one; it fires more specific rules (rules with more conditions).
glossary.sentence: This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A component where facts are stored with time tags; the inference engine can fire rules based on the data most recently entered here.
glossary.sentence: This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: Systems that use production rules for knowledge representation and can represent and reason with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based expert systems have the following advantages: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...” ( Negnevitsky , 2011)

glossary.definition: Additions used by most rule-based expert systems to represent and reason with incomplete and uncertain knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.

glossary.definition: A control cycle where the selected rule can be displayed at each stage.
glossary.sentence: At each stage of the control cycle "Recognize-act", the selected rule can be displayed.

3. NO external knowledge. Synthesize exclusively from source.
4. DO NOT translate. Keep definitions in the source language.
